*Only includes people in prison for new offenses and excludes people with life sentences and sentences less than one year.
::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} # Parole Eligibility ## How Parole Eligibility Is Determined Parole eligibility refers to the point in a person's prison sentence when they become eligible for consideration for release under parole. Parole is a conditional release, allowing the person to serve the remainder of their sentence in the community under supervision, rather than in prison. For most people who are incarcerated in Montana, first eligibility for discretionary parole release occurs at the 25-percent mark of their judicial maximum sentences. Sentencing courts in Montana have the unusual power to order in individual cases that people who are incarcerated will never enjoy eligibility for discretionary release or that their minimum terms to release eligibility will be delayed past the 25-percent mark of their maximum sentences. Montana is one of a few states that have abolished good time, earned time, and related credits for people in prison to reduce their judicial maximum sentences or the period of time to first parole-release eligibility.¹
## In Prison Past Parole Eligibility In 2019, 48 percent of people in prison were currently past their parole eligibility. Another 52 percent will reach their parole eligibility after 2019. ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
``` From 2010 to 2019, the percent of people in prison past parole eligibility increased by 4 percent. ```{=html}
```
### Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} #### Race and Ethnicity In 2019, 75 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were White, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` #### Sex In 2019, 94 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were male. ```{=html}
``` #### Age In 2019, 31 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
### Offense Types In 2019, 68 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility were in prison for violent offenses and 32 percent for nonviolent offenses. Most people who were incarcerated past parole eligibility were serving time for rape or sexual assault (29%) and aggravated or simple assault (20%) offenses. ```{=html}
```
### Sentence Lengths In 2019, 45 percent of people in prison past parole eligibility had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years. ```{=html}
```
## Estimation Methodology Parole eligibility year (PEY) estimates for 2020 and earlier are calculated in three steps. (1) We start with the PEY reported by National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP) selected variable series for the year we are focusing on.² (2) Next, because in states PEY is missing for people who should have one, perhaps replaced with a new date after a failed hearing, we look for the PEY for the same person in previous reports. (3) Because some earlier PEY might still not be identified, we then use the state's parole eligibility rules to create a conservative estimate of the PEY and replace the previous one if the rules-based estimate is earlier. Because the estimate is conservative, the result may be an undercount of the people past their PEY.

To match people to their PEY in in previous reports without an official identifier, we create one based on the variables that should be constant across reports: state, sex, race, admission year, admission type, age at time of admission, sentence length, max. release year, offense (detailed), and offense (general). If in any year multiple people have the same identifier, the latest PEY among them is selected, with missing PEY treated as later than any other year. If a previous report returns a PEY earlier than the one from the year we are focusing on, that one is selected. The process is repeated for each report back until 2000..

Rules-based imputation is based on rules identified by Reitz and colleagues.³ It is a conservative estimate computed as follows: First, for each person, it assigns a min. sentence based on the highest possible percentage of the max. sentence (100 percent for those who's most serious offense is non-violent, and 100 for those whose most serious offense is violent). All fractions are rounded up. If the rules-based imputation is earlier than the one found in the previous steps, it is used as the best estimate.

Estimates for the percentage of people past their PEY in 2023 are based on regression equation for the same variable between 2010 and 2020. The regression accounts for different trends each state has in terms of how much the percentage of people past their PEY increased or decreased annually in the previous decade and how much the percentage of people past PEY changed in 2020 due to special COVID policies. The total number of people past PEY in 2023 is the multiplication of the 2023[pending decision]population times the percentage of people past their PEY. [PENDING: explain the adjustment of pop. data from NCRP 2010-2020 and NPS 2010-2022, TBD]. Prison population beyond that in NCRP was obtained from the National Prisoners Survey (2010-2022) and the state's department of corrections' report (2023).⁴ ::: {.cell} ::: # Prison Population ## Prison Population Trends From 2010 to 2022, the prison population increased 16 percent, changing from 3,716 in 2010 to 4,313 in 2022. ```{=html}
```
### Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} #### Race and Ethnicity In 2020, 71 percent of people in prison were White, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` #### Sex In 2020, 86 percent of people in prison were male. ```{=html}
``` #### Age In 2020, 28 percent of people in prison were between the ages of 25 and 34 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
### Offense Types In 2020, 27 percent of people in prison were incarcerated for rape or sexual assault offenses. ```{=html}
```
### Sentence Lengths In 2020, 36 percent of people in prison had sentence lengths between 10 and 24.9 years. ```{=html}
``` ::: {.cell} ::: # Prison Releases ## Prison Release Trends From 2010 to 2019, people released from prison increased 6 percent, dropping from 1,206 in 2010 to 1,284 in 2019. ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
``` In 2019, 47 percent of people eligible for parole were released during their eligibility year. This represents a 35 percent increase compared to 2010. ```{=html}
```
## Conditional Releases Conditional release involves an individual’s release under specific conditions and supervision, whereas unconditional release means the individual is released without further obligations or restrictions. In 2019, 77 percent of people released from prison were conditional releases. ```{=html}
```
### Demographics ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs} #### Race and Ethnicity In 2019, 68 percent of people released from prison were White, non-Hispanic. ```{=html}
``` #### Sex In 2019, 86 percent of people released from prison were male. ```{=html}
``` #### Age In 2019, 33 percent of people released from prison were between the ages of 35 and 44 years old. ```{=html}
``` :::
### Offense Types In 2019, 25 percent of people released from prison were incarcerated for property offenses. ```{=html}
``` ::: {.cell} ::: # Disparities ## Disparities in Parole Release ::: {.panel-tabset .custom-tabs-disp} ### Race and Ethnicity ```{=html}
``` ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/pngs/pe_rri_infographic_black_Montana.png){width=1190} ```{=html} In 2019, Black people were 20 percent less likely to be incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility than White people, when accounting for population sizes in Montana. ``` ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/pngs/pe_rri_infographic_hispanic_Montana.png){width=1188} ```{=html} In 2019, Hispanic people were 50 percent less likely to be incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility than White people, when accounting for prison population sizes in Montana.
Hispanic RRI should be interpreted with caution due to inconsistencies in how each state collects and reports data on ethnicity. ``` ### Sex ```{=html}
``` ![](../../Users/mroberts/The Council of State Governments/JC Research - Documents/RES_Parole/data/analysis/app/pngs/pe_rri_infographic_male_Montana.png){width=1181} ```{=html} In 2019, males were incarcerated in state prison past parole eligibility at a rate 1.3 times higher than females, when accounting for prison population sizes in Montana.
``` :::

In 2019, Black people spent on average 0.4 less years past parole eligibility, and Hispanic people spent on average 1.6 less years past parole eligibility compared to White people.

In 2019, Black people spent on average 0.1 less years in prison, and Hispanic people spent on average 0.9 less years in prison compared to White people.

``` ```{=html}
``` ```{=html}
The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by offense type and race and ethnicity in 2020. The largest disparity was observed among robbery offenses, where Black people spent on average 1.6 more years in prison compared to White people.

The chart below shows the average time spent in prison past parole eligibility by offense type and sex in 2020. The largest disparity was observed among rape or sexual assault offenses, where males spent on average 1.8 more years in prison compared to females.

``` ```{=html}

### How Disparities Are Calculated
The Relative Rate Index (RRI) is a tool used to compare the rates at which different groups experience incarceration, parole eligibility, or other criminal justice system outcomes. In this report, we use the RRI to highlight disparities in parole release and incarceration across race, ethnicity, and sex. An RRI greater than 1 means that the group experiences the event at a higher rate, while an RRI less than 1 means they experience it at a lower rate. State data broken down by race, ethnicity, and sex were pulled from National Corrections Reporting Program, 1991-2020: Selected Variables (NCRP). Then, the rate of people readmitted to parole was calculated within each racial and ethnic group based on the Census Public Use Microdata Sample (PUMS). The relative rate index is calculated by dividing the rate for Black individuals or Hispanic individuals by the rate for White individuals. While White, Black, and Hispanic are not the only race and ethnicity categories found in the U.S., NCRP only provides four categories: White, Black, Hispanic, and Other. Further categories will be added in future data collections by the CSG Justice Center. Additionally, the term Hispanic is used here to maintain consistency with the data source material. States vary in how they collect and report information about race and ethnicity. Some states use the race or ethnicity recorded by intake officers, while other states allow individuals to identify their own race and ethnicity. Furthermore, some states combine race and ethnicity into one category, while others separate them into two distinct items. The U.S. Census allows individuals to self-identify their race and ethnicity separately. The variation in practices across states and between state and federal data reporting systems may result in a deflated RRI for Hispanic individuals. These differences can result in an incomplete count of Hispanic individuals and those of mixed race in state reports. Conversely, it can result in an overcount of people of other races, especially non-Hispanic White people. ::: ::: {.cell} :::
## Endnotes ¹ Kevin R. Reitz, Melanie Griffith, and Edward E. Rhine, *Prison-Release Discretion and Prison Population Size: State Report: Montana *(Robina Institute of Criminal Law and Criminal Justice, July 2023), [https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-08/montana_doi_report_7_30_23](https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2023-08/montana_doi_report_7_30_23).pdf

² US Bureau of Justice Statistics., "National Corrections Reporting Program NCRP Series" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research, 2022), accessed October 2, 2024, https://www.icpsr.umich.edu/web/NACJD/series/38.

³ Kevin Reitz et al., American Prison-Release Systems: Indeterminacy in Sentencing and the Control of Prison Population Size, Final Report (Minneapolis, Minnesota: Robina Institute, University of Minnesota, 2022), accessed October 1, 2024, https://robinainstitute.umn.edu/sites/robinainstitute.umn.edu/files/2022-05/american_prison-release_systems.pdf, 36-43.

⁴ US Bureau of Justice Statistics, "National Prisoner Statistics, 1978-2022" (Inter-university Consortium for Political and Social Research), accessed January 10, 2024, https://doi.org/10.3886/ICPSR38871.v1.“Daily Population Report – 12/31/2023,” Montana Department of Corrections, accessed October 1, 2024, https://dataportal.mt.gov/t/COR/views/POPReportPublic_15931787918580/SecurePopulation?%3Aiid=2&%3AisGuestRedirectFromVizportal=y&%3Aembed=y. Data analysis was conducted using information from the National Corrections Reporting Program and the National Prisoner Statistics Program. See “National Corrections Reporting Program (NCRP),” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp](https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-corrections-reporting-program-ncrp); “National Prisoner Statistics (NPS) Program,” Bureau of Justice Statistics, accessed September 25, 2024, [https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-prisoner-statistics-nps-program](https://bjs.ojp.gov/data-collection/national-prisoner-statistics-nps-program).
